elements.sent-2411-001: Energy transfer across scales is fundamental in fluid dynamics, linking large-scale flow motions to small-scale turbulent structures in engineering and natural environments.
elements.sent-2411-002: Triadic interactions among three wave components form complex networks across scales, challenging understanding and model reduction.
elements.sent-2411-003: We introduce Triadic Orthogonal Decomposition (TOD), a method that identifies coherent flow structures optimally capturing spectral momentum transfer, quantifies their coupling and energy exchange in an energy budget bispectrum, and reveals the regions where they interact.
elements.sent-2411-004: TOD distinguishes three components—a momentum recipient, donor, and catalyst—and recovers laws governing pairwise, six-triad, and global triad conservation.
elements.sent-2411-005: Applied to unsteady cylinder wake and wind turbine wake data, TOD reveals networks of triadic interactions with forward and backward energy transfer across frequencies and scales.
elements.sent-2411-006: Tones in spectra are a common feature of both natural and engineering flows, providing valuable insights into the extrinsic and intrinsic, as well as linear and nonlinear, dynamics of fluid systems.
elements.sent-2411-007: These tones can generally be classified into two categories: fundamental and nonlinear.
elements.sent-2411-008: Fundamental tones either arise from external influences or originate from intrinsic flow instabilities.
elements.sent-2411-009: External frequencies are prevalent in many technical applications, such as the blade-passing frequency in rotating machinery like turbines, fans, and propellers.
elements.sent-2411-010: In geophysical and atmospheric flows, tonal peaks corresponding to semi-diurnal, diurnal, and annual frequencies are driven by Earth's rotation, axial tilt, and orbital dynamics relative to the Sun and Moon.
elements.sent-2411-011: Hydrodynamic instabilities, such as Kelvin-Helmholtz waves in shear layers, represent the second source of fundamental tones.
elements.sent-2411-012: These tones can often be predicted by hydrodynamic stability theory as discrete eigenvalues of the linearized dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, even linearly stable flows can exhibit tones when forced near an eigenfrequency, a phenomenon known as linear resonance.
elements.sent-2411-014: Nonlinear tones, on the other hand, arise from the interactions of fundamental or other nonlinear tones.
elements.sent-2411-015: These originate from the quadratic nonlinearity of the convective term in the Navier-Stokes equations, which generates triadic sum- and difference-frequency interactions.
elements.sent-2411-016: These resonant triadic interactions produce peaks at sub-, super-, and ultra-harmonic frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-017: It is important to distinguish these from non-resonant triadic interactions, which only redistribute energy without producing distinct tones, as seen in the broadband spectrum of isotropic, homogeneous turbulence.
elements.sent-2411-018: While fundamental tones are often straightforward to identify by matching frequencies to known external sources or predictions from linear theory, nonlinear tones are typically identified by matching non-fundamental peaks in power spectra to integer multiples or sums and differences of fundamental frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-019: Since the power spectrum defines energy as the squared magnitude of the signal, it is inherently phase-blind.
elements.sent-2411-020: Thus, peaks at sub- and super-harmonic frequencies are a necessary, but not sufficient, condition for confirming nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-021: The occurrence of ultra-harmonic tones from triadic interactions between different spatial symmetry components adds further complexity to interpreting power spectra alone.
elements.sent-2411-022: A well-established tool for identifying triadic interactions in pointwise signals is the bispectrum, along with its normalized counterpart, the bicoherence.
elements.sent-2411-023: The bispectrum is a third-order statistic that correlates two frequency components with their sum, making it useful for detecting triadic frequency interactions that satisfy the zero-sum condition by analyzing the phase correlation among the three involved frequencies (see \citet{brillinger1965introduction for an early review).}
elements.sent-2411-024: Building on this, \citet{schmidt2020bispectral proposed bispectral mode decomposition (BMD), which uses an integral measure of bispectral density to identify coherent flow structures associated with triadic interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-025: BMD achieves this by maximizing the block-to-block correlation between the local product of two frequency components and their sum, determining the numerical radius of a spectral estimate of the bispectral density matrix.
elements.sent-2411-026: While BMD identifies a single mode pair representing the dominant triad at each point in the bispectrum plane, the method introduced in this work provides complete bases of convective modes and recipient modes, along with a modal energy budget that quantifies the scale-to-scale energy transfer.
elements.sent-2411-027: While the nonlinear term in the momentum equations can transfer and redistribute kinetic energy, it can neither produce nor remove net energy.
elements.sent-2411-028: The conservation of scale-to-scale kinetic energy transfer has been known at least as far back as the early text of \citet{Batchelor1953Cambridge , and was later expounded on by \citet{kraichnan1959jfm in the wavenumber domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: A systematic analysis of spectral energy conservation and transfer was also carried out by \citet{smyth1992pof , which is the foundation for this work.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The properties of inter-triad energy conservation are also treated in standard texts, including \citet{Lesieur1990Kluwer , \citet{schmidhenningson2001springer , and others.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: These properties are directly analogous in the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-032: Recent applications of kinetic energy transfer analysis and inter-triad conservation are found in the works of \citet{Barthel2022thesis and \citet{FreemanEtAl2024JFM .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The transport of kinetic energy in fluid flows is governed by the dynamical equations for the mean kinetic energy (MKE) and turbulent kinetic energy (TKE).
elements.sent-2411-034: These equations may be partitioned into terms that can be interpreted as distinct mechanisms for energy flow, see e.g. \citet{pope2000cambridge .}
elements.sent-2411-035: They include: production, dissipation, advection, and the transfer of energy through pressure, viscous diffusion, and nonlinear interactions.
elements.sent-2411-036: Depending on the flow, each term represents either a contribution of energy to, or a removal of energy from, the overall kinetic energy budget.
elements.sent-2411-037: In Fourier space, the spectral kinetic energy equation describes the transfer of energy across scales, from frequency to frequency, and wavenumber to wavenumber.
elements.sent-2411-038: One of the pioneering examples of spectral energy budget analysis of inhomogeneous flows is the work by \citet{lumley1964pof , which investigated the transfer of energy between wavenumbers in wall-bounded turbulence, and proposed the idea of an inverse energy cascade in which energy is transported from smaller to larger scales.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Similar observations about energy directivity were also made by \citet{Batchelor1953Cambridge .}
elements.sent-2411-040: More recently, the donor-recipient interpretation has been used to study energy transfer in turbulence by \citet{DomaradzkiRogallo1990POF , \citet{WebberEtAl2002IJNMF , \citet{Alexakis2005PRL , \citet{deWitEtAl2022JFM , \citet{DingEtAl2024arxiv , as well as in magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) turbulence by \citet{DarEtAl2001PhysD , \citet{AlexakisEtAl2005PRE , among others.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Whereas early studies of energy budgets tended to emphasize energy transfer as a local (pointwise) phenomenon, \citet{RempferFasel1994jfm demonstrated that it is possible to incorporate the concept of coherent structures into a framework of modal energy budget analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-042: A comprehensive summary of the methodology and application to a complex bluff-body flow can be found in \citet{hosseini2016modal .}
elements.sent-2411-043: In these works, coherent structures were educed via space-only POD and employed as the basis for a Galerkin reduced-order model (ROM) of the global energy dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-044: The model thus emphasizes the transfer of energy mediated by interactions between structures.
elements.sent-2411-045: While these modal bases optimally represent the observable dynamics or the linear input-output system, TOD modes are specifically designed to optimally account for triad interactions.
elements.sent-2411-046: This feature makes the latter ideally suited to the analysis of modal energy budgets in the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-047: Among the terms in the MKE and TKE budget equations, production, advection, and nonlinear transfer are triadic in nature.
elements.sent-2411-048: We show that these triadic energy budgets can be directly recovered from the convective modes and recipient modes of the proposed decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-049: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-050: First, \S \ref{sec:ortho_decomp introduces the governing equations for linear momentum and kinetic energy in the frequency domain, and the new orthogonal decomposition in \S\S \ref{subsec:eqns and \ref{sec:method , respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Thereafter, \S \ref{sec:modalEnergyFlow covers the modal energy flow analysis, starting with the theory of the spectral energy budget in \S \ref{subsec:budget and inter-triad energy conservation in \S \ref{subsec:conservation , before applying these concepts to modal decomposition in \S \ref{subsec:directivity .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Two applications of the decomposition are demonstrated in \S \ref{sec:applications : the cylinder wake at $\Re=100$ as a canonical example of a nonlinear laminar flow in \S \ref{sec:cylinder , and PIV data of a wind turbine wake as an example of a turbulent engineering flow in \ref{sec:windturbine in \S \ref{sec:cylinder .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Finally, \S \ref{sec:discussion discusses our findings and summarizes the method.}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Spectral Momentum and Kinetic Energy Equations
elements.sent-2411-054: The motion of an incompressible Newtonian fluid is governed by the momentum equations, \begin{equation}\label{mom_eqn} \pdv{\vb*u}{t} = -(\vb*u\vdot\grad)\vb*u - \grad p + \frac{1}{Re}\laplacian\vb*u,\end{equation} where $\vb*u = [u\;v\;w]^T$ the velocity vector field.
elements.sent-2411-055: Equations (\ref{mom_eqn ) have been nondimensionalized by the velocity scale, $U_\infty$, the length scale, $L$, and are parameterized by the Reynolds number, $\mathrm{Re =\frac{U_\infty L {\nu}$, where $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Assuming a time-periodic flow, the flow variables can be expanded as the Fourier series, \begin{equation}\vb*u(\vb*x,t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty \hat{\vb*u}_n(\vb*x)\ee^{\ii 2\pi f_n t} \qqtext{and} p(\vb*x,t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty \hat{p}_n(\vb*x)\ee^{\ii 2\pi f_n t}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: For each frequency, $f_n$, we obtain the frequency-domain representation of equation~\eqref{mom_eqn , \begin{equation}\label{spec_mom_eqn} \ii 2\pi f_n \hat{\vb*u}_n = -\qty(\widehat{(\vb*u\vdot\grad)\vb*u})_n - \grad \hat{p}_n + \frac{1}{Re}\laplacian\hat{\vb*u}_n = -\sum_{l=-\infty}^\infty \underbrace{(\hat{\vb*u}_{n-l}\vdot\grad)\hat{\vb*u}_l}_{-\vb*{\hat{c}}_{l\rightarrow n}} - \grad \hat{p}_n + \frac{1}{Re}\laplacian\hat{\vb*u}_n ,\end{equation} where in the second step we have invoked the convolution theorem.}
elements.sent-2411-058: This frequency-domain, or spectral, momentum equation lies at the core of our modal momentum transfer analysis.
elements.sent-2411-059: The special role and implications of the components in the convolution sum, $\vb*{\hat{c _{l\rightarrow n}$, are discussed in \S \ref{sec:recdon .}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: For the often-used notation of triads, $(k,l,n)$ with $k=n-l$, any two of the frequency components determine the remaining one.
elements.sent-2411-061: For simplicity, we use the pairwise notation $(l,n)$ to denote the triplet $(n-l,l,n)$ in the following.
elements.sent-2411-062: For the case of $n=0$, equation~\eqref{spec_mom_eqn simplifies to \begin{equation}\label{eq:RANS} 0 = -(\bar{\vb*u}\vdot\grad)\bar{\vb*u} -\sum_{l\neq0} \underbrace{(\hat{\vb*u}_{-l}\vdot\grad)\hat{\vb*u}_l}_{-\vb*{\hat{c}}_{l\rightarrow 0}} - \grad \bar{p} + \frac{1}{Re}\laplacian\bar{\vb*u} = -(\bar{\vb*u}\vdot\grad)\bar{\vb*u} - \overline{(\vb*u'\vdot\grad)\vb*u'} - \grad \bar{p} + \frac{1}{Re}\laplacian\bar{\vb*u},\end{equation} which are the Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations, with $\vb*u'\equiv\vb*u-\bar{\vb*u}$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The definition of $\vb*u'$ implies that $\hat{\vb*u _l=\widehat{\vb*u'_l}$ for $l\neq0$, which leads to the second equality in equation~\eqref{eq:RANS .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Each term in the RANS equations contributes to the deformation of the mean flow, $\bar{\vb*u}$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The term $\vb*{\hat{c _{l\rightarrow 0}$, in particular, is responsible for momentum transfer via the Reynolds stress, $\overline{(\vb*u'\vdot\grad)\vb*u'}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Upon left-multiplying with $\hat{\vb*u _n^{\mathrm{H }$, equation~\eqref{spec_mom_eqn can be rewritten as the spectral kinetic energy equation, \begin{equation}\label{spec_mom_eqn_mult} \ii 2\pi f_n\hat{\vb*u}_n^{H}\hat{\vb*u}_n = -\hat{\vb*u}_n^{H}\sum_{l=-\infty}^\infty\underbrace{(\hat{\vb*u}_{n-l}\vdot\grad)\hat{\vb*u}_l}_{-\vb*{\hat{c}}_{l\rightarrow n}} - \div(\hat{p}_n\hat{\vb*u}_n^{H}) + \frac{1}{Re}\hat{\vb*u}_n^{H}\laplacian\hat{\vb*u}_n,\end{equation} where we used expression \ref{eq:c for the convective term.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The spectral kinetic energy equation governs the distribution of the kinetic energy, $\hat{E _n=\frac{1 {2}\hat{\vb*u _n^{\mathrm{H }\hat{\vb*u _n$, and forms the basis for the modal energy flow analysis in \S \ref{sec:modalEnergyFlow .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: A wide variety of open flows exhibit strong convective instability.
elements.sent-2411-069: Examples encompass natural and technical flows, such as bluff-body wakes and boundary layers, as well as exogenously forced flows like plasma-actuated jets.
elements.sent-2411-070: In these flows, the convective term $\vb*{\hat{c _{l\rightarrow n}$ becomes dominant in both spectral momentum and kinetic energy equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: In the following, we outline how the spatial structures involved in spectral momentum transfer can be leveraged to identify and understand triadic flow interactions.

elements.sent-2411-219: For the cylinder wake, the vortex-shedding frequency is used as the fundamental frequency, and for the wind turbine wake, we use the rotor rotational frequency.
elements.sent-2411-220: For the first example, we systematically present the method's primary outcomes and illustrate the interpretation of the results.
elements.sent-2411-221: The second example provides a comprehensive analysis, focusing on the physical explanation of a complex technical flow.

elements.sent-2411-373: In this work, we have developed a novel modal decomposition which distills coherent flow structures that optimally account for triadic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-374: For a given triad, triadic orthogonal decomposition decomposes the convective and recipient terms in the momentum equations into a pair of jointly optimal orthogonal modal bases, ranked in terms of the covariance between the projections onto the respective bases.
elements.sent-2411-375: Maximizing the covariance also captures the bispectral statistics that emerge due to prevalent triadic interactions within the flow.
elements.sent-2411-376: By considering a three-wave interaction as the covariance between the convective and recipient terms, we show that the jointly optimal bases are given by the left and right singular vectors of a singular value decomposition of the convective-recipient covariance tensor.
elements.sent-2411-377: The strength of the correlation is given by the singular value, and measures the statistical prevalence of a given triad.
elements.sent-2411-378: The singular values of all triads are presented in the form of a mode bispectrum, providing a succinct indication of active triads.
elements.sent-2411-379: For nonzero recipient frequencies, $f_n\neq0$, the convective and recipient mode pair models the inter-scale momentum transfer as governed by the spectral momentum equations.
elements.sent-2411-380: For $f_n=0$, the mode pair instead contributes to the Reynolds stresses in the RANS equations, thereby acting on the mean flow.
elements.sent-2411-381: By relating the spectral momentum equations to the spectral kinetic energy equation, we show that a natural consequence of the scale-to-scale modal momentum transfer is the modal energy transfer.
elements.sent-2411-382: The latter is obtained from the singular value and the projection of the convective mode onto the recipient mode.
elements.sent-2411-383: We interpret the spatially-integrated modal energy transfer as a modal energy budget.
elements.sent-2411-384: This interpretation follows from recognizing that the convective term in the kinetic energy equation can be partitioned into spectral TKE contributions from linear advection by the mean, production by the mean, and nonlinear transfer, as well as MKE contributions from mean self-advection, production and nonlinear transfer.
elements.sent-2411-385: The relative importance of each of these budget terms to the overall energy dynamics can be directly read off the bispectrum plane.
elements.sent-2411-386: Specifically, MKE budget terms reside on the $f_n=0$ axis, while spectral TKE budget terms are active off that axis.
elements.sent-2411-387: Within the TKE budget, linear advection by the mean resides on the $f_n=f_l$ diagonal, and production by the mean is found on the $f_l=0$ axis.
elements.sent-2411-388: As required by the globally conservative nature of the convective term, the nonlinear energy transfer of individual triads, summed over the bispectrum plane, vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-389: Perhaps lesser-known is that triadic energy transfer is conserved also in a pairwise fashion.
elements.sent-2411-390: Since TOD modes form an optimal representation of the recipient and convective fields, under the condition of large separation between the optimal and suboptimal convective-recipient covariances, the integral modal energy transfer is also pairwise conservative.
elements.sent-2411-391: Taking advantage of pairwise conservation, we use the modal energy budget to systematically excavate and quantify the energy donated from an arbitrary frequency, $f_l$, and received by another arbitrary frequency, $f_n$, mediated by a catalyst frequency, $f_k=f_n-f_l$.
elements.sent-2411-392: Positive energy transfers (marked red in this work) in the region $f_n>|f_l|$, or equivalently, negative energy transfers (marked blue) in the region $f_n<|f_l|$, indicate a forward cascade of energy from low to high frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-393: Conversely, negative transfers in $f_n>|f_l|$, or equivalently, positive transfers in $f_n<|f_l|$, signify an inverse cascade of energy from high to low frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-394: It must be emphasized that finite correlation between the recipient and convective terms does not imply a causal association between the two.
elements.sent-2411-395: That is, the TOD mode bispectrum and modal energy transfer establish a statistical and energetic link between the donor, recipient, and catalyst frequencies, but not a causal link.
elements.sent-2411-396: TOD is therefore not equipped to resolve the questions of which frequency arises first, or which frequency generates which, nor is it obvious that such questions are in fact meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-397: Rather, in this work, a triad interaction is understood as the collective action of all three frequency components that contributes to the momentum and energy dynamics of a flow.
elements.sent-2411-398: Collectively, the scale-to-scale energy exchanges enabled by all donor-recipient pairs are woven into a network of energy transport.
elements.sent-2411-399: Energy enters and leaves the system through the $f_l=0$ axis and the $f_n=f_l$ diagonal.
elements.sent-2411-400: These represent respectively production and linear advection by the mean---linear mechanisms that are unrestrained by energy conservation.
elements.sent-2411-401: Once inside the system, energy is scattered over the nonlinear regions of the bispectrum plane, circulating from donor to recipient and bound by a hierarchy of conservative properties.
